It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>

The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>

[[en:Kumano Taisha]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kumano-taisha}} [[ja:熊野大社]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]] [[zh:熊野大社]]
{{wikidata link|Q11568887}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]].<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where [[:en:Control of fire by early humans|the use of fire]] originated; two ancient [[:en:Fire making|fire-making tools]], a [[:en:Bow drill|hand drill]] (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]], by [[:en:Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata no Orochi]] and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}.<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where {{ill|Control of fire by early humans|en|Control of fire by early humans|WD=Q912205|lt=the use of fire}} originated; two ancient {{ill|Fire making|en|Fire making|WD=Q1192478|lt=fire-making tools}}, a {{ill|Bow drill|en|Bow drill|WD=Q4115364|lt=hand drill}} (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}}, by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Yoshitoshi}}]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}} and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-taisha]][[en:Kumano Taisha]][[ja:熊野大社]][[simple:Kumano Taisha]][[zh:熊野大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568887]] Wikidata name: Kumano Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}.<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where {{ill|Control of fire by early humans|en|Control of fire by early humans|WD=Q912205|lt=the use of fire}} originated; two ancient {{ill|Fire making|en|Fire making|WD=Q1192478|lt=fire-making tools}}, a {{ill|Bow drill|en|Bow drill|WD=Q4115364|lt=hand drill}} (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}}, by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Yoshitoshi}}]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}} and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

The shrine is also considered in myth to be where {{ill|Control of fire by early humans|en|Control of fire by early humans|WD=Q912205|lt=the use of fire}} originated; two ancient {{ill|Fire making|en|Fire making|WD=Q1192478|lt=fire-making tools}}, a {{ill|Bow drill|en|Bow drill|WD=Q4115364|lt=hand drill}} (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>

The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.

On the right-hand side stands the Inada Shrine, which is dedicated to Princess [[Kushinadahime|Inadahime]], whom Susano-o-no-Mikoto saved from the 8-headed serpent {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}} and married, according to Japanese mythology. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union. It is believed to increase one's love fortune and bring about marital satisfaction.<ref>{{cite news |title=聖域開放、期間限定の熊野本宮大社参拝 » Lmaga.jp |url=https://lmaga.jp/news/2019/01/55186/ |work=Lmaga.jp |date=8 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=出雲大社に行こう |date=2013 |publisher=学研パブリッシング |isbn=978-4-05-610091-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzw4O40nAXYC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA72 |language=ja}}</ref>
==History== The Kumano Taisha has a long history dating back to ancient times. The shrine was first recorded in the Chapter 26: [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] chronicle (659 AD) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (The Chronicles of Japan). According to the ancient chronicles, Izumo no kuni fudoki (733 AD), the shrine was originally located on Mount Kumano (present-day Mount Tengu). The shrine was mentioned as Kumano Nimasu Shrine in the ''Engishiki Jinmyo-cho Notebook'' (927 AD) and was also referred to as Hinomoto-hidezome Shrine. Kumano Taisha Shrine has been considered a grand shrine of the Land of Izumo since ancient times, and it has been revered along with Izumo Taisha Shrine. The imperial court of Japan also deeply revered the shrine, and it rose in Rank from 3rd in 851 AD to 2nd in 867 AD respectively.<ref name="history"/>
The Kumano Taisha was relocated from Mount Kumano to the foot of the mountain. Subsequently, the shrine was split into an upper and a lower section. Until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the upper shrine was called Kumano Sansha Gongen, while the lower shrine was known as Isemiya. In 1871, the shrine was designated as a middle-Ranked shrine and was later elevated to the status of a top-Ranked Grand National Shrine in 1916. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}'s shrine system reform, its title was downgraded from Taisha (the grand shrine) to Jinja (a regular shrine). It wasn't until 1978 that it was finally reinstated to the Rank of Taisha. The same year saw the rebuilding of the shrine during the sengu ceremony, which also involved a reform of the buildings within the shrine grounds. Additionally, the Sukei-kai, an organization of local parishioners and believers from all over the country, was established. Kumano Taisha Shrine shares a strong connection with [[:en:Izumo Taisha Shrine|Izumo Taisha Shrine]] and the festival of Izumo Taisha begins with receiving the sacred fire, which is the essence of Kumano Taisha's deity, along with the fire-kindling tools from Kumano Taisha. These tools are also utilized in the inauguration ceremony at Izumo Taisha and are presented annually at Kumano Taisha's Sankasai Fire Festival.<ref name="history"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 熊野大社随神門.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate 熊野大社拝殿.JPG|The worship hall 熊野大社舞殿.JPG|The dance hall 熊野大社鑽火殿.JPG|The fire hall </gallery>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Taisha | native_name = 熊野大社 | image = Kumano taisha.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan#Japan Shimane Prefecture | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|37|33.2|N|133|07|03.2|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Kokuhei Taisha|Kokuhei Taisha]] | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] (some source says) | established = 7th century | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = Sankasai Fire Festival | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kumano Taisha'''|熊野大社|''Kumano-taisha''}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, Shimane Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to the god Kaburogi-kumano-okami Kushi-mike-no-mikoto (伊邪那伎日真名子加夫呂伎熊野大神櫛御気野命, "Beloved Child of Izanagi, Divine Ancestor [and] Great deity of Kumano, Kushimikenu-no-Mikoto'), which is identified with [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社｜東北の観光スポットを探す |url=https://www.tohokukanko.jp/attractions/detail_1267.html |work=東北の観光・旅行情報サイト「旅東北」 |language=ja}}</ref>
The Kumano Taisha is considered one of the most sacred ''[[ichinomiya]]s'' in the Izumo Province, along with the [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Grand Shrine]]. It is not to be confused with the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}.<ref name="history">{{cite book |title=原日本の精神風土 |date=8 July 2008 |publisher=NTT出版 |isbn=978-4-7571-4189-6 |page=74 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bVPvRxfF4AwC&dq=%E7%86%8A%E9%87%8E%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE&pg=PA74 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=熊野大社 |url=https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20304 |work=しまね観光ナビ｜島根県公式観光情報サイト |date=7 March 2020 |language=ja}}</ref> It is significant in Japanese culture as the origin of fire in Japan. The shrine is also considered in myth to be where {{ill|Control of fire by early humans|en|Control of fire by early humans|WD=Q912205|lt=the use of fire}} originated; two ancient {{ill|Fire making|en|Fire making|WD=Q1192478|lt=fire-making tools}}, a {{ill|Bow drill|en|Bow drill|WD=Q4115364|lt=hand drill}} (燧杵 ''hikiri-kine'') and a hearthboard (燧臼 ''hikiri-usu'') are kept in the shrine and used in the shrine's Sankasai Fire Festival (鑚火祭 ''Kiribi-matsuri'' or ''Sanka-sai'') held every October.<ref>{{cite web |title=熊野大社 (Kumano Taisha) |url=http://furusato.sanin.jp/p/area/matsue/34/ |access-date=2020-03-30 |website=山陰観光【神々のふるさと山陰】}}</ref>
==location== The shrine is situated in a mountainous region about 15km to the south of Matsue's central district and is accessible by car in approximately 30 minutes. The Yakumo-bashi Bridge, painted in vivid vermillion color, stretches across of Iu River. Before ascending the stone steps to the shrine, visitors can purify their body and mind at the water basin.
==Halls== [[File:Susanoo-no-Mikoto-slays-Yamata-no-Orochi-in-Izumo-By-Tsukioka-Yoshitoshi.png|thumb|left|Susanoo and the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi}}, by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Yoshitoshi}}]] In the center of the grounds lies the main hall, where the deity Susanoo-no-Mikoto is enshrined. On the left is Izanami Shrine dedicated to the creation goddess {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, who was the mother of Susano.<ref name="history"/>
In addition to the main hall, there are several other structures located in the grounds of Kumano Taisha. These include the Kou Shrine and Inari Shrine, as well as the Zuijin-mon Gate, Sanka-den Hall, the Mai-den spiritual dance hall, and a resting place called Kansui-tei. The comb guard at Kumano Taisha is well-known as a talisman for marriage. A comb-shaped amulet is given as a symbol of Kushinaida Hiyori and Motoen Kodori's successful union.
